subtitle: Participating in
title: Joint Quantum Lab
description: We develop scalable quantum photonic systems based on LC-SiN photonic integrated circuits.

sections.title: Joint Quantum Lab
content: We develop scalable quantum photonic systems based on LC-SiN photonic integrated circuits. In collaboration with KIST, we aim to establish an integrated research platform for the full-cycle design, fabrication, and evaluation of quantum photonic systems.

sections.title: What JQL is
content: The Joint Quantum Lab (JQL) is a collaborative research program designed to support researchers transitioning into quantum science and technology. It provides a platform where universities, national research institutes, and industry can build early-stage collaborations and carry out exploratory joint research. JQL promotes multidisciplinary research across materials, photonics, devices, packaging, and system integration. Through this program, our laboratory collaborates with KIST to develop scalable quantum photonic systems based on LC-SiN photonic integrated circuits.

sections.title: Overview
content: Photonic integrated circuits (PICs) are emerging as a core hardware platform for scalable quantum processors. LN platforms offer high-speed modulation, but scalable integration and packaging remain challenging. In SiN platforms, conventional thermo-optic tuning suffers from high power consumption, slow response, and thermal crosstalk.

sections.title: Our Approach
content: We develop LC-SiN PICs by introducing LCs as an active cladding layer above SiN waveguides. This hybrid structure combines the manufacturability of SiN photonics with the large optical anisotropy of LCs, enabling compact and low-voltage phase control. Based on our recent demonstration of wafer-scale fabrication feasibility, we are extending this platform toward packaged quantum photonic processor modules with multi-channel I/O.

subtitle: Toward device-level
title: Tactile displays
description: We develop deformable photonic crystal films based on chiral liquid crystalline elastomers

sections.title: Tactile displays
content: We develop deformable photonic crystal films based on chiral liquid crystalline elastomers (CLCEs). By integrating these films with addressable actuation platforms capable of driving individual pixels, we aim to realize large-area, state-memorizable tactile displays that can express both shape and color.

sections.title: What tactile displays are
content: Tactile displays deliver information through physical surface deformation, vibration, texture, or force feedback. Unlike conventional visual displays, they provide visual–tactile feedback by allowing digital information to be both seen and physically felt through a reconfigurable surface. Recent tactile interfaces have moved beyond simple vibration feedback toward shape-changing surfaces that can render buttons, patterns, textures, and three-dimensional topographies. This direction is especially important for tangible user interfaces, wearable devices, assistive technologies, and immersive interaction systems.

sections.title: High-sensitivity CLCEs
content: CLCEs form a self-organized helical structure that reflects selected wavelengths of light, producing structural color without dyes, pigments, filters, or backlight units. We have developed highly deformation-sensitive CLCE films in which even small local inflation can produce a large shift in reflected color across the visible wavelength range. This high deformation sensitivity allows modest tactile actuation to generate strong optical contrast while minimizing mechanical strain on the device.

sections.title: Our approach
content: To move this material platform toward practical devices, we pursue large-area and highly uniform CLCE films that can generate consistent color responses across an extended surface. This scalability allows deformation-sensitive photonic films to move beyond small laboratory samples toward realistic display components. By integrating the CLCE color layer with an addressable actuation substrate, each tactile pixel can locally deform the film and convert strain into visible color. Through this combination of scalable CLCE fabrication and individually addressable actuation arrays, we aim to control both shape and color across a large-area surface for tangible visual–tactile displays.

title: LC-based smart window
description: Adaptive photonic structures using mechanically responsive LC materials.
